other_observers: Cole Wild, Nick Komar, and many others; Nick Komar was the original finder and identifier of the bird
description: the bird was a small goose, about the size of the neary Richardson's Cackling Geese, but with a shorter, thicker neck, and shorter tail giving the bird a more compact appearance. The bill was black, and longer than on nearby Cackling and Lesser Canada Geese. The tail was much shorter, barely sticking out beyond the upper and undertail coverts.  The head of the bird was entirely black. The neck was also black, except for a conspicuous white marks at the top of the neck that almost met in the back of the neck. The bird's chest was black, contrasting with the dark brown on the sides and back of the bird, which was slightly darker than the brown on the same areas of Canada and Cackling Geese. There were some vertical white markings along the brown flanks of the bird. The flight feathers were blackish, with some white edging on the coverts forming white horizontal stripes on the folded wing.  The birds tail was black, but almost entirely obscured by the very long white uppertail coverts. The undertail coverts were white, contrasting sharply with the dark brown flanks and belly of the bird.Behaviors: the bird was mostly hanging out with Lesser Canada Geese on the pond, swimming around.Call: none heardPlumage: adult black
